metadata.title: Voce Chat - runs locally, chat anywhere
metadata.description: VoceChat is a lightweight self-hosted chat app, API and SDK. Deploy on Docker, NAS, or Raspberry Pi with full data ownership and no user limits.
metadata.supportDescription: Get help with VoceChat installation, hosting, customization, and technical support.
metadata.privacyDescription: Privacy policy for the VoceChat mobile application and related services by Privoce.
metadata.blogDescription: Curated articles, videos, and docs about VoceChat from across the web — with quotes and links to the originals.
metadata.keywords: VoceChat, self-hosted chat, private chat server, Docker chat, NAS chat, Rust IM, chat SDK, in-app messaging, team chat, open source chat

firstView.title: Personal Chat, Personal AI, Personal Cloud, all hosted on your PC !
firstView.subtitle: VoceChat is a superlight chat app! Host on your computer, access anywhere. Perfect for launching your AI agent or vibe coding project!

features.heading: Open API and SDK for you to host anywhere. No central service.
features.intro: Bots, video call, publish channel, open ID, open API, open sourced SDK, iterate based on users feedback. Trusted by over <count>40,000</count> customers from 50+ countries.
features.securityItem1: All data is stored on your own, private, server.
features.securityItem2: The server is developed in Rust with critical business process tests with 80%+ coverage.
features.lightweightItem1: The server is less than 20 MB in size and can easily run on a NAS and Raspberry Pi.
features.crossPlatformItem2: Support Android, iOS, MacOS, Web, and Windows platforms.
features.openapiItem1: Utilizes open API standard, with easy integration with third-party applications and tools.

faq.heading: FAQ
faq.subtitle: Self-hosted chat, bots, AI agents, webhooks, and video — based on doc.voce.chat and the VoceChat community.
faq.a1: VoceChat is a lightweight, self-hosted chat server built in Rust (about 20 MB). It offers DM and group chat, replies, @mentions, file sharing, burn-after-reading, an open API and SDK, bots with webhooks, and an embeddable website widget — all on infrastructure you control. The server is open source and designed for private cloud, NAS, homelab, and local-first deployment.
faq.a2Lead: The fastest way is Docker — one command starts the official image:
faq.a2Tail: Binary and source installs are also supported. See the <install>install guides</install> for TLS, reverse proxy, and upgrade steps.
faq.a2Schema: Install VoceChat with Docker: docker run -d --restart=always -p 3000:3000 --name vocechat-server privoce/vocechat-server:latest. See https://doc.voce.chat/install/
faq.a3: Anywhere you control. The simplest start is on your own computer — laptop or desktop — and VoceChat includes a built-in Cloudflare Tunnel that gives you a public link to your server, so you can chat from anywhere on the internet without opening router ports or buying a VPS. You can also host on a cloud VPS, a Synology or UGREEN NAS, or keep it on a private intranet for internal teams only. Pick local + tunnel for personal use, VPS or NAS for always-on communities, or intranet when you want zero public exposure. See the <install>install guides</install> for each option.
faq.a3Schema: Host locally on your computer with VoceChat's built-in Cloudflare Tunnel for internet access, or on a VPS, NAS (Synology/UGREEN), or private intranet. Local hosting provides a public tunnel link without port forwarding. See https://doc.voce.chat/install/
faq.a4: The VoceChat server itself is about 20 MB and runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, and ARM devices including Raspberry Pi. A VPS with 512 MB RAM or more is plenty for small teams. The official Docker image is roughly 24 MB. Clients are available for iOS, Android, desktop, and web.
faq.a5: Yes — the self-hosted community edition is free for servers with up to 20 members. Larger communities need a license. Pro unlocks unlimited members, more bots and webhooks, the website chat widget, and email notifications. There is no backdoor and no usage telemetry; license checks use local RSA verification.
faq.q6: How do Bots and Webhooks work?
faq.a6: Server admins create a Bot under Settings → Bot & Webhook, then generate an API Key. Use the key in the x-api-key header to send text, Markdown, or file messages to users and channels via the Bot API. Set a webhook URL on the same bot to receive inbound events — new messages, replies, edits, deletions, and new-user signups (for welcome messages). Webhooks can optionally use AES encryption. This is how teams build live-chat widgets, form-to-chat dashboards, newsletters, and automation backends. See the <bot>Bot & Webhook guide</bot> and <api>API docs</api>.
faq.a6Schema: Admins create bots at Settings → Bot & Webhook with an API Key for outbound messages (text, Markdown, files) and a webhook URL for inbound events. Supports welcome messages, optional AES encryption, and automation use cases. Docs: https://doc.voce.chat/bot/bot-and-webhook and https://doc.voce.chat/api-doc
faq.q7: Can I connect VoceChat to AI agents and OpenClaw?
faq.a7: Yes. VoceChat is built to be a chat frontend for agents. For a custom AI bot, point a bot webhook at your backend (OpenAI, local LLM, or any service) and reply through the Bot API — see the <gpt>GPT bot example</gpt>. For OpenClaw, use the official VoceChat plugin: OpenClaw listens on port 8010, receives VoceChat webhook POSTs, routes them to your agent runtime, and sends replies back via the Bot API — supporting private chat, groups, text, replies, and file uploads. Popular setups include personal AI assistants, local LLM APIs + VoceChat, and OpenClaw-powered bots on your own machine. Read the <openclaw>OpenClaw integration guide</openclaw>.
faq.a7Schema: VoceChat supports AI via bot webhooks and the Bot API (see GPT example at https://doc.voce.chat/bot/demo-gpt). OpenClaw integrates through the VoceChat plugin on port 8010 for bidirectional private and group chat. Guide: https://doc.voce.chat/openclaw-integration
faq.q8: Does VoceChat support video and voice calls?
faq.a8: Yes, in two ways. <vocespace>VoceSpace</vocespace> lets you self-host branded video and audio conferencing under your own domain with enterprise-grade control — ideal for client-facing chat + video solutions. For the self-hosted server, you can also enable Agora WebRTC in Settings: Agora offers a generous free tier (about 167 hours/month shared across all callers on your server). Start a call from the icon in any chat window.
faq.a8Schema: Video/voice via VoceSpace (self-hosted branded conferencing at https://vocespace.com) or Agora WebRTC configured in VoceChat settings (~167 free hours/month). Docs: https://doc.voce.chat/setting/setting-agora
faq.q9: What can I build with VoceChat beyond team chat?
faq.a9: Community and GitHub use cases include: website live-chat and contact widgets, webhook dashboards for static-site form submissions, internal company messaging, public event boards on landing pages, NAS bookmark and file sharing, private social feeds, customer-support bots, bridges toward Discord or Slack, and in-app messaging via the open API and SDK. Embed the <widget>chat widget</widget> on any site with a few lines of JavaScript.
faq.a9Schema: Use cases include website widgets, webhook automation, internal messaging, event boards, support bots, and in-app messaging via API/SDK. Widget docs: https://doc.voce.chat/widget
faq.a10: VoceChat has no backdoor and collects no chat data on Privoce servers — everything stays on your instance. Traffic can use encrypted transmission; messages support burn-after-reading. Webhook payloads can be AES-encrypted. Being self-hosted means you own storage, access control, and retention policies.
faq.a11: Start with <link>doc.voce.chat</link> (install, bots, API, OpenClaw, widget). Browse our curated <blog>community guides</blog>, search or file issues on <github>GitHub</github> (vocechat-server, vocechat-web), email han@privoce.com, or visit the <support>support page</support> for hosting and customization help.
faq.a11Schema: Help: https://doc.voce.chat/, community guides at https://voce.chat/blog, GitHub issues at https://github.com/Privoce/vocechat-server, email han@privoce.com, and https://voce.chat/support
faq.blogCta: See tutorials, reviews, and deployment guides from across the web.
